content.full_text:
air Political Practices CommissioIL 
Ms. Arline Ray Polgar 
10391 Ballard Avenue 
Garden Grove, CA 
92640 
Dear Ms. Polgar: 
1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
-
-
October 19, 1976 
Mr. David Shapiro has referred your question concern-
ing how loans must be reported under the provisions of the 
Political Reform Act, to us for a response. 
A loan which is made to a candidate or committee for 
use in a political campaign, is treated as a contribution 
to the campaign in the event it is forgiven by the lendor. 
The loan would not be listed as income as defined in 
Section 82030 unless it is clear from the surrounding 
circumstances that the loan was made for personal and not 
political purposes. 
I trust this answers your question, if you desire 
additional information, please do not hesitate to call me. 
RSK:dw 
Very truly yours, 
Regina Siciliano-Kutchins 
Technical Assistance Progarm 
Coordinator 


DAVID I. SHAPIRO 
11905 KUNG STREET, APT. 6 
NORTH HOLLYWOOD, CALIFORNIA 91607 
(213) 763-8938 
October 
7th 
1 976 
Mr. John Keplinger 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K Street 
Sacramento, California 
Dear Mr. Keplinger: 
Last weekend I participated in a USC forum at which Dan 
Loewenstein was a featured speaker. I told him of my interest in 
serving on your staff. He answered that although there were no 
openings currently I should apply to you. 
I am enclosing my resumes. My value lies in my eagerness for 
the Reform Act to work, having assisted in its enactment, mixed with 
empathy with those, like myself, who have to live under its mandates 
and strictures. 
P. S. 
DIS:pl 
Encl: 
Sincerely, 
Arline Ray Polgar, 10391 Ballard Avenue. Garden 
Grove, California 92640 (714) 6;J6-79I7, handed me 
a question during our workshop to present to Mr. 
Loewenstein. He in turn, asked me to write it. She 
wants to know whether or not a campaign loan, not 
forgiven, is treated as "contribution" or "income" 
under 8~015 or 8~030 of the Political Reform Act. 
Please mail 
answer to her. Thanks for your time 
in this regard.

sections.question: She wants to know whether or not a campaign loan, not forgiven, is treated as "contribution" or "income" under 82015 or 82030 of the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion: A loan which is made to a candidate or committee for use in a political campaign, is treated as a contribution to the campaign in the event it is forgiven by the lendor. The loan would not be listed as income as defined in Section 82030 unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that the loan was made for personal and not political purposes.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a campaign loan that is not forgiven should be reported as a contribution or as income under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: An unforgiven campaign loan is generally not treated as a contribution unless forgiven; it should not be listed as income under Section 82030 unless circumstances indicate it was made for personal rather than political purposes.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (Shapiro's letter with referral) and FPPC response (Siciliano-Kutchins' letter). Extracted information from the FPPC response portion. Question text reconstructed from the postscript of Shapiro's referral letter since formal question section was not present in response letter itself.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: She wants to know whether or not a campaign loan, not forgiven, is treated as "contribution" or "income" under 82015 or 82030 of the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: A loan which is made to a candidate or committee for use in a political campaign, is treated as a contribution to the campaign in the event it is forgiven by the lendor. The loan would not be listed as income as defined in Section 82030 unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that the loan was made for personal and not political purposes.

embedding.summary: FPPC response to a question about loan reporting requirements under the Political Reform Act, clarifying that forgiven campaign loans are treated as contributions while unforgiven loans are typically not reported as income unless made for personal purposes.
